title: Elon Musk Keynote Address - AIAA 2011 (part 1/4)
text:
oh boy good afternoon and welcome back
for those that were here this morning
and looks like we have a few more to
join us if you're not here this morning
my name is Wayne herds from Northrop
Grumman systems corporation here in
southern california and i am the
industry general chair for this year's
joint propulsion conference after this
morning sessions i hope you all agreed
that we're off to a good start i was
happy to see full sessions papers being
presented the great keynote and panel
session this morning and one of the
things that made that possible was the
great help from our organizing committee
i want to once again thank my jpc
government co-chair Jimmy kenyon from
the office of the assistant secretary of
defense for research and engineering our
general chair for this year's I ecec Ted
Stern from Vanguard space technologies
and Ashwin Gupte from the University of
Maryland our current director for a
double a propulsion and energy group as
I said this morning we got a lot of help
pulling together this year's program and
once again I would like to thank all the
volunteers including the conference
technical chairs topic area organizers
and are sponsoring societies the a
double h staff and our industry sponsors
as well who made this all possible so
continuing our jpc theme design test fly
turning propulsion ideas to reality our
keynote speaker and panel this afternoon
will continue today's theme of space and
high-speed systems tomorrow again we
will turn our attention to propulsion
for aviation and Wednesday we'll talk
public policy and our test
infrastructure in parallel sessions
continuing on will be our I ACEC theme
continuing to offer topics on government
academia and industry working together
towards clean energy so there's lots of
topic areas for everyone to participate
in so you don't want to hear me speak
anymore so now to introduce our keynote
speaker for this afternoon I invite to
the podium our past Aila president and
the Willis young professor and chair for
the department of aerospace engineering
at the University of Maryland
dr. Mark Lewis
well thank you everyone and welcome to
what is already turning out to be a
spectacularly successful joint
propulsion conference I know none of you
are actually here to listen to me this
this afternoon so I'm going to make my
remarks as briefly as possible and those
remarks will be to introduce this
afternoon's guest speaker mr. Elon Musk
truly an individual who requires no
introduction but please indulge me
because I'm going to offer him one
anyway Elon Musk was I think you know is
the the CEO and the inspirational force
behind the company called SpaceX which
has made quite a name for itself he has
a very very impressive background born
and raised in South Africa he was the
son of a South African engineer his
mother was from Canada at the age of 17
he decided America was the land of
opportunity so on his own moved to
Canada in the United States a live with
family did a variety of odd jobs
including working on a wheat farm and
cleaning grain bins was accepted in 1992
to university of pennsylvania's wharton
school and also studied physics at the
university of pennsylvania from there he
went to graduate school at Stanford
where he explains that he basically
spent two days as a graduate student and
decided that now there are better things
in life went on to and which some of us
in academia would hardly agree with
don't quote that in front of any of my
graduate students went on to found a
company called zip to which provided
online content publishing software for
news organizations sold that company and
whereas I think most of us in this room
would have retired went on to found an
even bigger and better company paypal
which I'm sure many of you familiar with
Elon sold paypal and I think he decided
all he ever really wanted to do was
build electric cars and build space
vehicles and he's managed to be able to
do both in June of 2002 he founded space
explorations technology SpaceX he's
currently the CEO and the CTO very
impressive dual role and of course
SpaceX develops and manufactures space
launch vehicles perhaps their best-known
products or the Falcon one rocket and
most recently the Falcon 9 rocket he is
also a famous for his role in leading
the Tesla Motors Corporation of building
electric cars and
perhaps the first truly operation
electric cars that I think been an
inspiration to the rest of the
automotive industry and he is also the
primary investor in chairman of the
board of a Solar City it's a photocall
day its products and services company
that is providing electric power
services of planning to do so throughout
the United States if I went through a
list of awards and recognitions it would
also i think phil most of the alot of
time i'll just hit some highlights in
February 2011 Elon was listed as one of
America's 20 most powerful powerful CEOs
14-under he was listed as one of time
magazine's 100 people who will most
affect the world in 20 and 10 he is a
recipient of our very own ad the ballet
George Lowe astronautics award presented
for the most outstanding contributions
in the field of space transportation he
got that in 2007 2008 and he's on the
Caltech board of trustees perhaps most
impressive at least in my mind is in
2010 a survey by the Space Foundation
ranked Elon as the number tenth most
popular space hero tied with dr. Verner
von Braun he's also i think is very
well-known the inspiration for the
fictional character of Tony Stark in the
iron man 2 movie so without further ado
let me ask you to join me in welcoming
Elon Musk to the podium
I think thanks very much for having me
and for the kind introduction I think
we've got a video that may be a good way
to kick it off just to play the video if
we could
5 4 3 2 1
lift it off
Delphine
so I like to just give it a little bit
of background as to you know what why
I'm doing this I think I think some
people may be of heard part of the story
before so i apologize if so it seems
repetitive but I i started SpaceX nine
years ago almost exactly 99 years maybe
two months ago and the the reason for it
was was was I was really concerned that
we were not on a path to become a
multi-planet species in a spacefaring
civilization and you know if you look at
what our capability has been at various
points in time and you extrapolate that
it doesn't go in a good direction I mean
in 1969 we were able to go to the moon
and then in the 80s we were just able to
go to low Earth orbit with people and
and of course now we cannot even go to
low Earth orbit with people although
hopefully in a few years we'll be able
to do that on on our spacecraft or maybe
maybe other spacecraft other American
spacecraft but clearly something needs
to be done to reverse that trajectory or
or the future of space is extremely
dismal so that was the reason I started
SpaceX with that aspiration and but it
was a very difficult thing because I'd
never built anything physical before I'd
done a lot of software actually
technically had built Rockets when I was
a kid um with myth with mixed success in
South Africa that they didn't have stds
rockets so in order to make a rocket you
have to have to make sure in grain and
and you know get get all these
ingredients and and you know it's
actually pretty hard to create a solid
rocket propellant and get it all right
so it's some of them worked but apart
from like stuff as a kid that's the
first time I've made anything physic
is with SpaceX and so I had to kind of
figure out what all the elements of of
what going to a rocket you know how do
you make the engines in structure and
the avionics and the guidance of control
in the lad so took a little time to
figure that out and then we reached over
it with the Falcon one basically but you
know five and a half or what were ya
ghous it would have been six years after
starting the company so that took took a
while but since then our momentum has
increased quite a bit and then last year
we got what falcon line two orbits and
and then to drag into orbit and brought
it back safely and then hopefully if all
goes well this year we'll be docking
with the space station in December so
we're focused on that that's a that's
our current to the top priority and and
then okay and our other big priority of
course is making sure that we can
satisfy the Air Force in our 044 Space
Launch and in fact after we get to space
station that will be our top priority is
really really convincing the Air Force
and nro that we're a good vehicle
